projects.subtitle: Case studies focused on architecture, trade-offs, and operational behavior.
chaos.title: Chaos Playground
chaos.subtitle: Break this system. Watch it recover.
chaos.disclaimer: Chaos scenarios are intentionally triggered in a controlled environment to demonstrate resilience patterns.
chaos.narrative: Designed to survive burst traffic without degrading latency. Rate-limited, idempotent, and observable by design.
metrics.telemetry.title: Telemetry Timeline
metrics.failure_model.title: Failure Model
chaos.action.spike.title: Traffic Spike
chaos.action.spike.desc: Fires a burst of 30 concurrent requests. Observe latency spike in metrics above.
chaos.action.failure.title: Force Failure
chaos.action.failure.desc: Triggers a 503 condition. Watch system status shift to degraded.
chaos.action.cache.title: Cache Stress
chaos.action.cache.desc: 10 parallel reads. Client-side simulation — generates real backend load.
chaos.action.drain.title: Queue Drain
chaos.action.drain.desc: Force purges queued requests to simulate shedding load during backpressure.
chaos.action.retry.title: Manual Retry
chaos.action.retry.desc: Simulates a retry from exponential backoff rules after earlier degraded states.
chaos.action.latency.title: Latency Injection
chaos.action.latency.desc: Injects 3000ms latency to test timeout configurations and open the Circuit Breaker.
chaos.action.cache.disclaimer: Simulated via concurrent requests, not a backend-tracked incident.
chaos.note: Each action directly impacts the metrics visible above.
observability.title: Observability & Tracing
observability.subtitle: Every request can be inspected through structured logs and execution traces.
observability.empty: Trigger a chaos action to see traces appear here.
logs.title: System Logs
logs.subtitle: Structured telemetry reflecting real-time decisions, state transitions, and recovery attempts.
logs.empty: No log entries yet. Interact with the system to generate events.
about.title: Backend Engineering Focus
about.subtitle: Backend-oriented development focused on APIs and production operations.
about.bio: Interest in performance, resilience and observability. Building systems that fail gracefully, recover automatically, and expose everything through structured telemetry.
chaos.incidents.history_subtitle: Archived post-mortems from real production incidents. The failed diagnoses and wrong assumptions are documented alongside the fixes.
metrics.system_model.title: System Model
metrics.strategy.title: Adaptive Strategy
tradeoffs.title: Architecture Trade-offs
tradeoffs.subtitle: Engineering isn't about choices; it's about compromises.
tradeoffs.protip: Toggle [STRESS] or [FAILURE] mode below to see these trade-offs in action. When latency exceeds 100ms, the system automatically prioritizes availability by switching to the async fallback path.
tradeoffs.latency.title: Latency vs. Consistency
tradeoffs.latency.desc: Using stale-while-revalidate for metrics dashboard to prioritize UI smooth interaction over real-time accuracy during burst traffic.
tradeoffs.latency.impact: UI remains smooth under burst traffic. Cost: Reviewers may see slightly stale telemetry (P95 vs Current) during revalidation windows.
tradeoffs.sync.title: Sync vs. Async Processing
tradeoffs.sync.desc: Chaos actions are processed synchronously in the control panel but reflected asynchronously in the observability stream to mimic real-world distributed system behavior.
tradeoffs.sync.impact: Immediate UI feedback for chaos actions. Cost: Increases backend complexity; if the worker crashes, the observability stream lags behind the real state.
tradeoffs.logging.title: Structured vs. Text Logging
tradeoffs.logging.desc: Sacrificing human readability in raw streams for key=value formatting to ensure O(1) parsing by automated SRE tools.
tradeoffs.logging.impact: O(1) parsing for SRE tools. Cost: Sacrifices manual grep performance for machine-parsable accuracy.
chaos.presets.title: Chaos Execution Mode
chaos.presets.desc_off: System running in NORMAL production mode.
chaos.presets.mild_effect: → Effect: sporadic 100ms latency spikes added to telemetry burst.
chaos.presets.stress_effect: → Effect: +1 extra retry requirement · 15% request failure probability.
chaos.presets.failure_effect: → Effect: Critical degradation · backoff x2 · forced fallback activation.
incident.001.title: The Resetting Rate Limiter
incident.001.t2_desc: Container restart zeroed counters in production.
incident.001.t3_desc: Anti-spam protection bypassed via container cycles.
incident.001.postmortem_desc: First attempt: I increased the in-memory TTL to 24h thinking the counter was expiring too fast. The counter still zeroed on every restart. Wrong diagnosis: I assumed this was a configuration issue. It wasn't. Real cause: Koyeb's free tier uses ephemeral containers that wipe all in-memory state during inactivity restarts. The rate limiter worked perfectly — it just never survived the container lifecycle. Fix: Migrated to Redis (Upstash) for persistent state. Side effect: Redis is now critical path; if it fails, the limiter fails open to avoid blocking legitimate users.
incident.001.lesson_desc: "Redis is now in the critical path — if Upstash is down, the rate limiter fails open to avoid blocking legitimate users."
incident.001.debt_item1: Migrated rate limiting storage to Redis (Upstash).
incident.001.debt_item2: Implemented fail-open pattern for external dependencies.
incident.002.title: The Warm Cold Start
incident.002.t1_desc: P95 exceeded 300ms on initial data requests.
incident.002.t3_desc: Connection pooling overhead identified as root cause.
incident.002.postmortem_desc: First attempt: I reduced the keep-alive interval to 10m thinking the container was cold-starting. The container was warm; latency didn't drop. Wrong diagnosis: I assumed container cold start. It wasn't. Real cause: PostgreSQL → Supabase connection round-trip added ~250ms overhead to every initial read. The pooling wasn't being reused between requests. The bottleneck was infra-level, not application-level. Fix: Migrated static reads to in-memory JSONRepository with stale-while-revalidate pattern. Updated keep-alive to hit /health (no-DB path). Trade-off: Slight staleness during revalidation windows.
incident.002.lesson_desc: "Availability is a feature, but consistency is a requirement. Falling back to Cache Serving was the correct trade-off."
incident.002.debt_item1: Migrated static reads to memory-cached JSONRepository.
incident.005.title: The Monorepo Build Context
incident.005.t1_desc: Docker build failing to find local dependencies.
incident.005.t2_desc: Work directory forced to /backend in config.
incident.005.t3_desc: Absolute paths broken in CI runner filesystem.
incident.005.postmortem_desc: First attempt: Forced 'Work Directory: /backend' in Koyeb config. Not only did this fail to fix the build — it made things worse. The Dockerfile used relative paths that were now incompatible with the CI runner filesystem. Second attempt: Tried aligning the Dockerfile to work from /backend. This broke local builds. Wrong diagnosis: I treated this as a path issue in one environment. It wasn't. Real cause: Inconsistent build context between local, GitHub Actions, and Koyeb. No single source of truth for the build root. Fix: Standardized context to repository root across all environments. Prefixed all paths in Dockerfile (COPY backend/requirements.txt). Accepted side effect: Local builds must now also run from repo root.
incident.005.lesson_desc: "The build context must reflect the repository root to ensure portability across local, CI, and cloud environments."
incident.005.debt_item1: Synchronized Docker context at repository root.
incident.005.debt_item2: Refactored Dockerfile to use relative path mapping.
